overview.argument: The Psalter is the inspired prayer- and praise-book of the people of God — one hundred and fifty poems in which the whole life of faith finds its voice. It is not an argument but a symphony, gathered into five "books" (each closing with a doxology) and framed with deliberate care: it opens with two gates — Psalm 1, the two ways of the righteous and the wicked, and Psalm 2, the LORD and his Anointed — and it ends in an unbroken crescendo of hallelujahs (Psalms 146–150). Between them lies every note the believing heart can sound: lament and complaint, penitence and confession, thanksgiving and trust, wisdom and instruction, royal and messianic hope, and pure adoration. The movement of the whole is from the crisis of a threatened covenant toward the certainty that the LORD reigns — so that the Psalter as a book teaches faith to travel from honest lament all the way home to praise.
overview.occasion: The Psalms were composed across nearly a thousand years — from Moses (Psalm 90) through the great Davidic collection, the guild-poets Asaph and the sons of Korah, Solomon, and on to the singers of the return from exile (Psalm 137). Many carry superscriptions tying them to David and to named moments of his life, and roughly half the Psalter is ascribed to him. The individual poems were sung in temple worship, then gathered and shaped — over generations and into their final five-book order in the post-exilic period — into the book Israel and the church have prayed ever since. Their setting is therefore both intensely personal and fully corporate: the cry of one sufferer and the song of the whole assembly.
overview.theology: The Psalms hold the widest theology in the most personal key. God is King, Refuge, Shepherd, Rock, Judge, and Savior; the world is his creation and the Torah his gift (Psalm 19). Here the covenant with David is celebrated and, in the exile, agonized over (Psalm 89) before Book IV answers with the enthroned LORD (Psalms 93–99). The lament psalms give faith permission to protest and to wait; the penitential psalms (Psalm 51) teach true repentance; the imprecatory psalms hand vengeance over to God rather than the self; the wisdom psalms set the two ways before us. Above all the Psalter models a whole life turned Godward in speech — the school, as the fathers said, in which the Spirit teaches us to pray.
overview.christ: No Old Testament book is quoted more often in the New, and none was more on the lips of Jesus, who prayed the Psalms in life and in death (Psalm 22:1; Psalm 31:5). The church has always read them as the prayers of Christ and of his body: he is the true King and Son of Psalm 2, the righteous sufferer of Psalm 22, the one not abandoned to the grave of Psalm 16, the priest-king after Melchizedek of Psalm 110 (the most-cited verse in the New Testament), and the rejected stone become the cornerstone of Psalm 118. Luther called the Psalter "a little Bible," and Augustine heard in it, throughout, the one voice of the whole Christ — head and members — crying to God.

structure.label: The two gates — the Psalter's doorway
structure.gist: The two ways (Ps 1) and the LORD's Anointed (Ps 2) frame all that follows.

structure.label: Book I — the Davidic core
structure.gist: Mostly David; the righteous sufferer amid enemies, ending in blessing and doxology.

structure.label: Book II — David and the sons of Korah
structure.gist: Longing, deliverance, and kingship, closing with the royal Psalm 72.

structure.label: Book III — the covenant in crisis
structure.gist: Asaph and Korah; the problem of the wicked and the exile, ending in the dark Psalm 89.

structure.label: Book IV — the LORD reigns
structure.gist: The "Moses" book answers the crisis: God is king from everlasting to everlasting.

structure.label: Book V — the ascent to praise
structure.gist: Thanksgiving, the Songs of Ascents (120–134), and the great acrostic to God the King.

structure.label: The final Hallel — let everything praise the LORD
structure.gist: Five hallelujah psalms bring the whole Psalter to its close in unbroken praise.

external.name: Derek Kidner
external.note: Concise, theologically alert readings attentive to each psalm's movement and its place in the whole.

external.name: John Goldingay
external.work: Psalms (Baker Commentary on the OT Wisdom & Psalms, 3 vols)
external.note: Close attention to the Hebrew poetry and the honest range of Israel's prayer, lament included.

external.name: Nancy deClaissé-Walford, Rolf A. Jacobson & Beth Tanner
external.note: Reads the psalms both individually and as a shaped book with a canonical storyline.

external.name: Willem A. VanGemeren
external.note: A thorough evangelical exposition tracing structure, genre, and theology psalm by psalm.

external.name: Bruce K. Waltke & James M. Houston
external.work: The Psalms as Christian Worship / … Lament
external.note: Joins careful exegesis to the church's history of praying the Psalms as worship.

external.name: C.S. Lewis
external.note: A lay reader's honest wrestling with the Psalter's beauty, its judgment, and its praise.
